claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 5 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 5 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions about your situation.

**Let's start with your child:**

How old is the child you're claiming Child Benefit for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know which age category applies to your child. If you have multiple children you'd like to claim for, we can go through each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh, I'd like to claim for all three of my kids actually. Alex is 5, Blake is 17, and Charlie is 19.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great! Let's assess eligibility for all three of your children: **Alex (5)**, **Blake (17)**, and **Charlie (19)**. I'll go through each one.

---

**Starting with Alex (age 5):**

Since Alex is under 16, they automatically meet the age requirement. Let me check the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For **Alex (5)**: Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week, or not in paid work at all? 

(Given Alex is only 5 years old, I assume they're not working - but please confirm!)

---

While you answer that, I should note that for **Blake (17)** and **Charlie (19)**, since they're both 16 or over, I'll need to ask about their education or training status. 

Are Blake and Charlie:
- In **full-time non-advanced education** (e.g., A-levels, T-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs up to level 3)?
- In **approved training** (such as a government-funded traineeship in England, or Employability Fund programme in Scotland)?
- Or have they left education/training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Oh yes, Alex definitely isn't working - he's only 5!

As for the older two - Blake left school and isn't doing anything at the moment. But Charlie is still studying, doing his A-levels actually.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Let me continue the assessment for each child.

**For Alex (5):** Great, since Alex isn't working, let me check the next requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex:** Is Alex **not** receiving any benefits in their own right (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

(Again, at age 5, I'd assume not - but please confirm!)

---

**For Blake and Charlie**, let me check the pathways for their situations:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on what you've told me:

- **Blake (17)** has left school and isn't doing anything at the moment. Did Blake leave education recently? If so, there may be an extension period that could apply. **When did Blake leave school?**

- **Charlie (19)** is doing A-levels, which is full-time non-advanced education. That's promising for eligibility! I'll check the specific education requirements for Charlie once we finish with Alex and Blake.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Alex doesn't get any benefits - he's only 5, so no.

Blake left school back in the summer, so that's been a few months now. He hasn't signed up with careers service or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying!

**For Alex:** Let me continue checking the requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you! Now let me continue with Alex, and I have the information I need for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex:** Is Alex in any kind of apprenticeship? (I assume not at age 5!)

---

**Regarding Blake (17):** Based on what you've told me, Blake left school "back in the summer" (a few months ago) and hasn't registered with a careers service. 

The rules allow for an **extension period of up to 20 weeks** after leaving education, but **only if** the young person has registered with a qualifying body (like a careers service, Connexions, or similar). Since Blake hasn't signed up with careers service or anything like that, unfortunately Blake wouldn't qualify for this extension period.

This means Blake would likely be **ineligible** for Child Benefit unless they either:
- Start approved education or training (at least 12 hours per week of supervised study), or
- Register with a qualifying careers/training service

---

Once you confirm about Alex not being in an apprenticeship, I'll continue with his assessment and then we'll check Charlie's A-level situation properly.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
No, Alex isn't in an apprenticeship - he's only 5!

So does that mean Blake doesn't qualify then? That's a shame. What about Charlie though - he's doing his A-levels?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: You're right, of course Alex isn't in an apprenticeship at 5! Let me continue with Alex's assessment, and then we'll look at Charlie.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check residency requirements. This applies to you as the claimant (for all three children).

**Do you meet any of these UK residency requirements?**

- You **normally live in the UK**, OR
- You have **settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme, OR
- You have **pre-settled status** and are working/studying/seeking work, OR
- You're a **Crown servant** living abroad

The most common is simply that you normally live in the UK. Do you live in the UK?

---

**Regarding Blake:** Yes, unfortunately Blake doesn't currently qualify because they've left education and haven't registered with a careers service. However, if Blake decides to either:
- Return to approved education/training (at least 12 hours per week), or
- Register with a careers service while looking for work or training

...then you could potentially claim again for Blake. It's worth keeping in mind!

---

**For Charlie:** Once we confirm your residency, I'll check Charlie's situation. A-levels are on the approved list for Child Benefit, so that's looking positive! I'll just need to verify a few more details about the course.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

That's good to know about Blake, thanks. And yes, let's check Charlie's situation properly then!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, living in Manchester means you meet the UK residency requirement. Let me continue the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now, are you responsible for the children?**

This means either:
- You **live with** the children, OR
- You **contribute to their upkeep** (e.g., paying for food, clothes, housing, pocket money, etc.)

Do Alex, Blake, and Charlie live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, all three of them live with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue checking the requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for any of your three children?**

For example, is there another parent or guardian who might be claiming for Alex, Blake, or Charlie?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for any of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Are any of the children currently in any of the following situations?**

- In **hospital** for an extended stay
- In **local authority care**
- In a **foster care** arrangement

Or are they all living at home with you under normal circumstances?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They're all living at home with me under normal circumstances - none of them are in hospital, care, or fostered.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Final question:** Is the local council **NOT** paying towards any of your children's accommodation or maintenance?

In other words, you're supporting them yourself without council payments for their upkeep?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - I'm supporting them myself. The council isn't paying anything towards their accommodation or maintenance.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! That completes the assessment for Alex.

Now let me properly check **Charlie's situation** since he's 19 and doing A-levels. I need to go through the specific education requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great news! A-levels are on the approved list. I just need to confirm a few details about Charlie's studies:

1. **Is Charlie studying at least 12 hours per week of supervised study?** (i.e., full-time education)

2. **Is the course funded by an employer or linked to a future job with an employer?** (This would disqualify it)

3. **Is Charlie married or in a civil partnership?** (If so, they would need to be financially dependent on you)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Yes, Charlie is doing full-time A-levels - definitely more than 12 hours a week of supervised study at college.

No, it's not employer funded or anything like that - it's just normal college A-levels.

And no, Charlie isn't married or in a civil partnership.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great news for Charlie! His A-levels meet all the education requirements. Now I need to check the remaining criteria for Charlie (the same ones we checked for Alex).

**For Charlie (19, doing A-levels):**

1. **Is Charlie working less than 24 hours per week** (or not working at all)?

2. **Is Charlie NOT receiving any benefits in their own right** (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)?

3. **Is Charlie NOT in any kind of apprenticeship?** (A-levels at college wouldn't be an apprenticeship, but just confirming)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Charlie isn't working at all - he's just focusing on his A-levels.

No, he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

And no, he's not in an apprenticeship - just doing A-levels at college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That covers everything I need to know about Charlie. He meets all the requirements for the 16-19 education pathway.

Let me now provide you with the final eligibility outcome for all three children:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Child Benefit Eligibility Results

Here's the summary for your three children:

| Child | Age | Eligible? | Reason |
|-------|-----|-----------|--------|
| **Alex** | 5 | ✅ **YES** | Automatically qualifies as under 16 |
| **Blake** | 17 | ❌ **NO** | Left education and not registered with careers service |
| **Charlie** | 19 | ✅ **YES** | In approved full-time education (A-levels) |

---

### Summary

You are **eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex and Charlie**, but **not currently for Blake**.

---

### What to do next

**For Alex and Charlie:**
- You can apply for Child Benefit online at [gov.uk/child-benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit)
- You'll need your National Insurance number and the children's birth certificates

**For Blake:**
If Blake's situation changes, you may be able to claim for him. He would qualify if he:
- Starts **approved education or training** (at least 12 hours/week of supervised study), such as A-levels, T-levels, NVQs up to Level 3, or an approved training programme
- Note: Since Blake is 17, he still has time to re-enter education before turning 20

---

### Important note about High Income Child Benefit Charge
If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may have to pay back some or all of the Child Benefit through the High Income Child Benefit Charge. You can still claim, but it's worth checking if this applies to you.

---

Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify about your Child Benefit eligibility?

reasonings: Alex (age 5) qualifies for Child Benefit because: 1) He is under 16 years old, meeting the age requirement automatically. 2) He is not working (under 24 hours/week requirement met). 3) He is not receiving any benefits in his own right. 4) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. 5) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester). 6) The claimant is responsible for Alex (lives with him). 7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. 8) Alex is living at home under normal circumstances. 9) The local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 17) does NOT qualify for Child Benefit because: 1) He is aged 16-19, so must be in approved education/training OR registered with a careers service during the 20-week extension period. 2) Blake has left school and is not currently in any education or training. 3) Blake has not registered with a careers service or similar qualifying body. 4) Several months have passed since leaving school in summer, and without registration with a careers service, the extension period does not apply. Therefore, Blake does not meet the qualifying child criteria.

Charlie (age 19) qualifies for Child Benefit because: 1) He is aged 16-19 and in approved non-advanced education. 2) A-levels are on the approved qualifications list. 3) He is studying full-time (more than 12 hours/week of supervised study). 4) The course is not employer-funded or linked to a future job. 5) Charlie is not married or in a civil partnership. 6) He is not working (under 24 hours/week requirement met). 7) He is not receiving any benefits in his own right. 8) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. 9) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester). 10) The claimant is responsible for Charlie (lives with him). 11) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie. 12) Charlie is living at home under normal circumstances. 13) The local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for 2 out of 3 children. Alex (5) qualifies automatically as an under-16 child. Charlie (19) qualifies as he is in approved full-time non-advanced education (A-levels). Blake (17) does not currently qualify because he has left education and has not registered with a careers service, so does not meet the requirements for the 16-19 age group. The claimant meets all other requirements: UK residency, responsibility for the children, no competing claimants, and no council payments for the children's upkeep.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 5) qualifies for Child Benefit because: 1) He is under 16 years old, meeting the age requirement automatically. 2) He is not working (under 24 hours/week requirement met). 3) He is not receiving any benefits in his own right. 4) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. 5) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester). 6) The claimant is responsible for Alex (lives with him). 7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. 8) Alex is living at home under normal circumstances. 9) The local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 17) does NOT qualify for Child Benefit because: 1) He is aged 16-19, so must be in approved education/training OR registered with a careers service during the 20-week extension period. 2) Blake has left school and is not currently in any education or training. 3) Blake has not registered with a careers service or similar qualifying body. 4) Several months have passed since leaving school in summer, and without registration with a careers service, the extension period does not apply. Therefore, Blake does not meet the qualifying child criteria.

Charlie (age 19) qualifies for Child Benefit because: 1) He is aged 16-19 and in approved non-advanced education. 2) A-levels are on the approved qualifications list. 3) He is studying full-time (more than 12 hours/week of supervised study). 4) The course is not employer-funded or linked to a future job. 5) Charlie is not married or in a civil partnership. 6) He is not working (under 24 hours/week requirement met). 7) He is not receiving any benefits in his own right. 8) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. 9) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester). 10) The claimant is responsible for Charlie (lives with him). 11) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie. 12) Charlie is living at home under normal circumstances. 13) The local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for 2 out of 3 children. Alex (5) qualifies automatically as an under-16 child. Charlie (19) qualifies as he is in approved full-time non-advanced education (A-levels). Blake (17) does not currently qualify because he has left education and has not registered with a careers service, so does not meet the requirements for the 16-19 age group. The claimant meets all other requirements: UK residency, responsibility for the children, no competing claimants, and no council payments for the children's upkeep.
